mechanism: Flat scale-leaf vortex + heavy branch mass sweep in storm wind
ecosystem: Old-growth forest (Cascades, Olympic interior, Pacific Northwest)
status: Second consecutive wind entry; second entry in meteorological arc 249-252

tree_facts.taxonomy_note: Not a true fir. Pseudotsuga = 'false hemlock.' Three-pointed bracts on cones. Unique genus.
tree_facts.leaf_structure: Flat scale-leaves, pectinate (comb-like) arrangement, ~2-3 cm. Flat cross-section creates different vortex pattern than circular Sitka spruce needles.
tree_facts.branch_mass: Large, heavy branches that sweep in wind -- adds low-frequency component absent from Sitka spruce

acoustic_physics.vs_sitka_spruce: Flat needles create broader-spectrum vortex vs. Sitka circular needles; branch mass adds low-frequency sweep; irregular old-growth canopy creates turbulence that broadens spectrum
acoustic_physics.harmonic_profile: Multi-harmonic: energy at multiple frequencies from needles (high), twigs (mid), branches (low), canopy gaps (turbulence). Richer than Sitka spruce pure moan.
acoustic_physics.vertical_dimension: 70-90m canopy height: wind velocity varies with height, meaning different canopy layers produce different aeolian frequencies simultaneously.
acoustic_physics.E_comparison: E=5 vs. E=4 (Sitka spruce): branch mass movement adds energy to sound profile
acoustic_physics.cathedral_acoustics: Large straight trunks act as reverb chamber; sound enriched by distance traveled through canopy

geographic_context.original_range: ~8-10 million acres in Washington State alone; now 10-15% remaining
primary_locations: Olympic National Park interior (upper Hoh, Elwha, Dungeness valleys)
Mount Rainier National Park (Carbon River, Ohanapecosh)
Gifford Pinchot National Forest wilderness areas
Coast Range fragments (SW Washington, NW Oregon)
geographic_context.loss: 85-90% of original old-growth logged, predominantly 1880-1990. Acoustic landscape fundamentally transformed.

cultural_significance.indigenous: Old-growth Douglas fir as landscape itself for coastal and interior Salish, Chinook, and others. Sound is constant feature of homeland, not novel.
cultural_significance.timber_era: PNW timber industry defined by Douglas fir harvest; logging transformed acoustic landscape as thoroughly as any event since glaciation
cultural_significance.conservation: Northwest Forest Plan 1994; spotted owl controversy; old-growth protection battles partly motivated by non-timber values including sound
cultural_significance.acoustic_ecology: R. Murray Schafer, World Soundscape Project. Old-growth fir wind as keynote sound -- background against which other sounds heard. Pre-logging: omnipresent background of PNW forest.

theory_nodes.concept: 249 vs 250 acoustic contrast: Sitka spruce (circular needles, stiff branches, coastal, E=4, pure moan) vs. Douglas fir (flat leaves, large branch mass, old-growth interior, E=5, multi-harmonic resonance). Genuine acoustic difference from structural biology. Engine documents both because they are genuinely different sounds.

Old-growth acoustic ecology: 85-90% of original PNW old-growth logged (1880-1990). The sound at degree 250 can now only be heard in ~10-15% of original range. R. Murray Schafer: old-growth wind as keynote sound -- the omnipresent background of the original PNW acoustic landscape, now nearly silenced from most of its range.

cross_references.relationship: Consecutive wind entries. Structural acoustic contrast: circular vs. flat needles, stiff vs. sweeping branches, coastal vs. interior, E=4 vs. E=5. Together constitute the wind chapter of the meteorological arc.
Meteorological arc continues: wind (249, 250) → rain (251). The atmosphere producing sound in different forms.

s36_pair.pairing_note: E=5 vs E=7. Multi-harmonic resonance from forest vs. multi-harmonic guitar. Both produce complex harmonic spectra from physical structures (forest canopy / guitar string) driven by external force (storm wind / musician). The parallel is deep: both are instruments producing chords.
